qa_table.notes: Attio help center shows any user can generate an API access token self-serve via Workspace settings > Developers, and Attio has a free plan with self-serve signup.
Official docs state 'Attio provides a public REST API which exchanges JSON over HTTPS'; no GraphQL API found in official docs (a third-party blog claiming GraphQL is not corroborated).
Attio docs confirm two auth paths — workspace access tokens (API keys, sent as Bearer) and OAuth 2.0 with a token endpoint at app.attio.com/oauth/token — matching all three listed methods.
Self-serve API keys on a free plan, public REST docs, and an official MCP server support build_now.
Found no gating for basic API access; only note is OAuth is required if publishing a multi-workspace integration, which is not a blocker for building.
Fetched the cited page directly; it describes Attio's own hosted MCP server at mcp.attio.com/mcp, confirming official_mcp.
Copper support docs indicate API access is available on trial, Professional, and Business plans — so a free trial gives API access but it is plan-limited, matching the enum.
Developer docs describe a JSON-over-HTTPS REST API with standard HTTP methods and per-minute rate limits.
Copper developer docs confirm token-based API key auth (X-PW-AccessToken headers) plus OAuth 2.0 for partner integrations.
API-key builds work on a trial/paid plan, but the OAuth quickstart confirms client credentials must be obtained by emailing partners@copper.com, so build_with_limits fits.

qa_table.original: OAuth2 app registration gated behind emailing partners@copper.com; API requires Professional+ plan or trial
qa_table.verified_value: OAuth2 app registration gated behind emailing partners@copper.com; API requires Professional+ plan or trial
qa_table.notes: Quickstart literally links app registration to mailto:partners@copper.com for client_id/client_secret, and support docs confirm the trial/Professional/Business plan gate — both claims check out.
Fetched the cited page: Apideck (a third-party unified-API vendor, not Copper) offers an MCP server covering Copper; found no official Copper MCP, so community/third-party is correct.
Pricing page has no self-serve signup or free trial; every plan's 'Get started' button routes to demo scheduling, confirming a sales-led motion.
API reference lists resource-based endpoints (accounts, issues, attachments, /me) over HTTPS with bearer auth — a REST API.
Pylon docs show API tokens generated in the dashboard and passed as 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' — an API key scheme, matching the original.
API tokens are generated inside the dashboard, which requires a paid, sales-onboarded account (no trial/free tier), so needs_partner_or_paid_access is right.

qa_table.original: No self-serve or trial account; credentials require paying customer via sales-led annual contract
qa_table.verified_value: No self-serve or trial account; credentials require paying customer via sales-led purchase (Starter/Professional also offer monthly billing)
qa_table.notes: Substance is correct (no self-serve/trial, paying customer required); the 'annual contract' detail is slightly imprecise since Starter and Professional tiers show monthly billing options, with only Enterprise listed as yearly-only.
Fetched the cited page: Pylon's own docs describe its hosted MCP server at mcp.usepylon.com with OAuth 2.0, confirming official_mcp.
Docs show tokens can only be created inside an existing Gladly instance by a user granted 'API User' permission; there is no public/self-serve signup, and Gladly sells via demo/sales.
Gladly's developer portal documents a REST API at developer.gladly.com/rest; no GraphQL surface found.
Gladly REST docs confirm user-associated API tokens passed via Basic Authentication in the Authorization header — both listed methods describe the actual scheme.
API credentials require an existing customer org plus admin-granted API User permission, so building requires paid/partner access.

qa_table.original: Credentials require paid sales-led customer account; no self-serve signup
qa_table.verified_value: Credentials require paid sales-led customer account; no self-serve signup
qa_table.notes: Consistent with docs: tokens are created in-app by designated users of an existing Gladly instance; no developer sandbox or public signup found.

Multiple searches found no official Gladly MCP announcement and no community GitHub server; the only hit is a speculative third-party article discussing what a Gladly MCP could look like, supporting no_mcp_found.

Lark's open platform lets any Lark account (free plan available) create custom apps and obtain app credentials via the developer console with no sales gate; verified via docs flow rather than an explicit pricing statement.
Lark Open API is an HTTP/JSON server API where tokens go in request headers of HTTP calls (documented cURL examples) — a REST surface.
Lark docs confirm tenant_access_token/app_access_token credentials sent as 'Authorization: Bearer ...' and user_access_token obtained via standard OAuth 2.0 — both listed methods verified.
Self-serve developer platform, documented OAuth2/Bearer REST API, and an official MCP server together support build_now.
No access gate found for building custom apps; only routine caveats (per-tenant app installation/permission scopes), which do not rise to a blocker.
Verified via GitHub API that the repo exists under the official larksuite org with description 'Lark official OpenAPI MCP', ~757 stars, not archived.
Self-serve signup with free credit and no card, but trial accounts can only SMS/call the registered and verified test numbers — exactly trial_or_free_but_limited.
Vonage's communications APIs (SMS, Messages, Verify, Voice) are documented as REST APIs on developer.vonage.com, with SDKs layered on top.
Official auth concepts page confirms API key/secret via Basic auth and JWT (RS256) as the main methods; digest auth exists only for SIP trunking, so the original is substantively correct.
Self-serve signup, free credit, public REST docs, and official SDKs mean an integration can be built immediately.
Only caveat is trial-mode restrictions on destination numbers until the account is topped up, which is self-serve to remove; 'none' is reasonable.
Repo exists under the official Vonage GitHub org (hosted at documentation-mcp.vonage.dev); note it is a documentation/code-snippet MCP rather than an API-action MCP, but 'official_mcp' is accurate.

Docs confirm apps managing other people's ad accounts need Advanced Access to ads_read/ads_management via App Review, i.e., platform approval; dev-mode-only use is self-serve but production requires approval.
Marketing API is part of the HTTP Graph API, conventionally classified as REST; no GraphQL/SDK-only surface contradicts this.
Marketing API authorization docs reference the OAuth dialog and access tokens (sent as bearer tokens on Graph API calls), matching OAuth2 + Bearer token.
You can build immediately in development mode with Standard access against your own accounts, but Limited-tier rate limits and App Review gating for external accounts justify build_with_limits.

qa_table.original: Meta App Review + Advanced Access (business verification) required to act on external ad accounts
qa_table.verified_value: Meta App Review + Advanced Access (business verification) required to act on external ad accounts
qa_table.notes: Docs explicitly state managing other people's ad accounts requires Advanced Access to ads_read/ads_management via App Review, confirming the blocker.
Meta's own announcement confirms an official Ads MCP server (endpoint mcp.facebook.com/ads) launched April 29, 2026 as part of Meta Ads AI Connectors.
API keys are self-generated from the account dashboard (Settings > MCP & API keys) with no approval process, and systeme.io offers a free plan.
Docs state the API follows RESTful conventions with an OpenAPI spec, JSON, cursor pagination, and merge-patch PATCH support.
Official developer docs confirm API key auth via the X-API-Key header, generated in dashboard settings; no OAuth for the public API.
Self-serve keys make it buildable today, but rate limits and an API that covers only part of the platform support build_with_limits over build_now.

qa_table.verified_value: API surface is partial but broader than contacts/tags: official endpoint index lists contacts, tags, funnels, newsletters, payments/subscriptions, courses, communities, and webhooks
qa_table.notes: The official endpoint index shows roughly ten resource groups (funnels, courses, communities, payments, webhooks, etc.), contradicting the 'mainly contacts/tags' characterization even though the surface is still partial.
Official help center and developer.systeme.io/docs/mcp-server document a vendor-provided MCP server using dedicated MCP keys.

Credentials require Account Manager with Account Administrator/SLAS admin roles tied to a licensed B2C Commerce instance; there is no public self-serve signup or free sandbox, so partner/customer gating is accurate.
SCAPI (and legacy OCAPI) are REST/JSON APIs per the official Commerce API guide.
Shopper APIs use SLAS (OAuth2 flows issuing JWT access tokens) and Admin APIs use Account Manager OAuth2 client credentials, matching OAuth2 + JWT.
Consistent with the licensed-instance and Account Manager admin requirements; nothing is buildable without customer/partner credentials.

qa_table.original: Credentials require licensed B2C Commerce instance and Account Manager/SLAS admin setup; no self-serve sandbox
qa_table.verified_value: Credentials require licensed B2C Commerce instance and Account Manager/SLAS admin setup; no self-serve sandbox
qa_table.notes: Docs confirm SLAS admin setup needs an AM token, Account Administrator role, and SLAS Organization Administrator roles scoped to specific B2C instances.
taurgis/sfcc-dev-mcp is a community-maintained (non-Salesforce) MCP server for SFCC development with docs and log-analysis tools; no official Salesforce B2C Commerce MCP found.
Docs say keys are copied from the FanBasis dashboard API Keys section with no approval workflow mentioned, though you must first be an onboarded FanBasis merchant.
REST/JSON API under /public-api/ with standard HTTP verbs, covering checkout sessions, subscriptions, customers, refunds, discounts, and webhooks.
Public API reference states every request must include the API key in the x-api-key header; no OAuth or bearer tokens.
Self-serve API keys and a public REST reference make it buildable, with limits from the payments-only scope and needing a FanBasis merchant account first.

qa_table.original: Small payments-focused API from young vendor; dev portal login-gated; account requires merchant onboarding
qa_table.verified_value: Small payments-focused API from young vendor; dev portal login-gated; account requires merchant onboarding
qa_table.notes: Confirmed the API is payments/checkout-focused (merchant of record model), dev-docs.fanbasis.com presents a login page, and enterprise pages funnel to discovery calls; note apidocs.fan itself is publicly readable.

No FanBasis-hosted or dedicated community MCP is documented on fanbasis.com or apidocs.fan; only Zapier's auto-generated aggregator MCP wrapper exists, so no_mcp_found is defensible though borderline.
Docs state the API is 'available on eligible paid plans' with only a small set of free test queries for development, confirming paid gating.
API v3 is a REST API with an OpenAPI-compatible specification per the official reference introduction.
Official docs confirm API keys created in Account settings; API v3 keys are sent as Authorization Bearer headers (the hosted MCP additionally uses OAuth sign-in).
API access requires an eligible paid plan and consumes paid API units, so nothing meaningful is buildable without paying; verdict is consistent.

qa_table.original: API keys gated behind paid subscription; usage consumes paid API units
qa_table.verified_value: API keys gated behind paid subscription; usage consumes paid API units
qa_table.notes: Docs confirm both halves: API available only on eligible paid plans and queries consume API units (with limited free test queries).
Ahrefs publishes an official MCP (ahrefs/ahrefs-mcp-server repo and hosted remote server at api.ahrefs.com/mcp/mcp) documented in the cited help article.

The only true data API (Enterprise People & Company API) is Enterprise-only via sales; webhook ingestion is available self-serve on the paid Explorer plan, so paid_plan_required would also be defensible, but contact_sales matches the API-proper story.
Clay's docs state it has no traditional public API; the general mechanism is webhook-in to tables plus HTTP-out actions, with only a limited Enterprise-gated lookup API.
Clay webhooks use an auth token passed in the request header (shown once at creation) and the Enterprise API is key-based; 'API key' is a fair substantive label.
Webhooks require Explorer plan or above and the real lookup API requires Enterprise, so paid/partner access is genuinely needed to build.

qa_table.original: No general-purpose public API — programmatic enrichment gated to Enterprise; else webhook-in/HTTP-out around Clay tables
qa_table.verified_value: No general-purpose public API — programmatic enrichment gated to Enterprise; else webhook-in/HTTP-out around Clay tables
qa_table.notes: Matches Clay's own docs exactly: no traditional API, Enterprise-only People/Company API, webhook-in plus HTTP-out as the workaround.
Fetched Clay's own MCP page; it is a first-party Clay MCP connector for ChatGPT/Claude/Codex with 500 free credits on first connection.
Access tokens are created directly in account settings on any account including free Hobby, with no approval step.
Docs state all endpoints live under https://api.vercel.com and follow REST architecture.
Vercel API auth is a self-created access token sent as Authorization: Bearer; 'API key' is a loose synonym for the same token, so substantively correct.
Free self-serve account, instant token creation, documented REST API and official MCP — nothing gates immediate building.
Found no access gate; only standard rate limits apply, which do not rise to a blocker.
Fetched the page: Vercel MCP is described as 'Vercel's official MCP server', a remote OAuth MCP at https://mcp.vercel.com.
Confirmed self-serve 30-day trial (no card required) after which the account is suspended unless converted to paid consumption.
Snowflake publishes a documented REST API (plus SQL API and drivers/SDKs), so REST is the right primary classification.

Docs list key-pair JWT, OAuth, programmatic access tokens (PAT, sent as Bearer), and workload identity federation; original's items all map correctly (PAT is 'programmatic' not 'personal', a wording nit only).
Buildable today on the free trial but account suspends after 30 days/credit depletion without payment, so build_with_limits fits.

qa_table.original: Paid consumption platform after 30-day trial; account-specific hostnames; heavy credential setup
qa_table.verified_value: Paid consumption platform after 30-day trial; account-specific hostnames; heavy credential setup
qa_table.notes: Trial suspension after 30 days confirmed; REST endpoints are per-account URLs and auth requires key-pair/OAuth/PAT setup, matching the claim.
Fetched the cited page: Snowflake-managed MCP server is a first-party product exposed at an account HTTPS endpoint with OAuth 2.0.
API tokens are generated by any user from account settings with no approval or paid plan required; found no contradicting evidence.
Docs state 'The Coda API is a RESTful API that lets you programmatically interact with Coda docs.'
API reference (coda.io/developers/apis/v1, now redirecting to docs.superhuman.com after the Superhuman/Grammarly merger) confirms an API token sent as 'Authorization: Bearer'.
Self-serve token plus documented REST API means immediate buildability; the MCP correction does not change the verdict.
No access gate found; worth watching that coda.io API docs now redirect to docs.superhuman.com post-merger, but the API remains live and open.
Coda now publishes an official first-party MCP server (beta) at https://coda.io/apis/mcp per its help center, which supersedes the community orellazri/coda-mcp classification.
Tokens and OAuth2 clients are created self-serve in the Harvest ID developer portal with no approval step; Harvest itself has a free trial/free tier signup.
Harvest API v2 is a documented JSON REST API at api.harvestapp.com/v2 with standard resource endpoints.
Official API v2 docs list exactly two methods: personal access tokens from Harvest ID Developers section, and self-registered OAuth2 apps (auth code and implicit flows).
Self-serve signup, instant PAT creation, and open REST docs mean an integration can be built immediately.
Found no gating beyond normal rate limits; 'none' is accurate.
The cited Harvest Help Center article (direct fetch 403'd but content confirmed via search) documents a first-party Harvest MCP for timers, expenses, and timesheets.
Docs confirm users create API keys themselves on the account API Management page with no approval step (note: geographic restrictions apply, e.g. US users must use Binance.US).
Confirmed REST API at api.binance.com; a WebSocket API also exists but REST is the primary documented surface and the enum has no WebSocket option.
Official docs confirm API key plus signed requests; HMAC is the primary scheme though RSA and Ed25519 keys are also supported, so the original is substantively correct if slightly incomplete.
Self-serve keys, public REST docs, and existing community MCP support building today.
No access blocker found in docs; only minor caveats (KYC account required, regional restrictions) that do not block a standard build.
Repo exists and self-describes as an unofficial community Binance MCP implementation; a search for an official Binance MCP announcement found only third-party servers.
Paygent's site routes prospects to an inquiry form or sales phone line and gates developer docs behind a contracted-merchant support site.
Site describes module-embed and link (ASP) integration types but publishes no public API spec, so the surface type cannot be confirmed publicly.
Official site exposes no public auth documentation (third-party GitHub integrations suggest merchant ID/connect ID plus client certificate, but no official public spec), so 'unclear' is fair.
Integration requires a Japanese merchant contract with Paygent before docs or credentials are provided, matching the verdict.

qa_table.original: No public developer docs or self-serve credentials; requires Japanese merchant contract with Paygent; product identity ambiguous
qa_table.verified_value: No public developer docs or self-serve credentials; requires Japanese merchant contract with Paygent
qa_table.notes: Confirmed: contracted developers are directed to a gated merchant support site and prospects must contact sales; blocker description is substantively accurate.
GitHub/web search for a Paygent MCP server returned no results (nearest hit was unrelated 'payware'), confirming no MCP found.

Public API is 'available for all Enterprise workspaces' (contact account manager if not visible), i.e. gated to the top paid plan; paid_plan_required is substantively right, arguably shading toward contact_sales since Enterprise itself is sales-sold.

Otter's official 'Otter.ai Public API' help article (updated April 2026) documents REST endpoints (e.g. GET /conversations with cursor pagination, rate limits, webhooks), contradicting No_public_API_found.

Otter now documents a Public API authenticated with self-generated API keys via Bearer token (created under Integrations > Developer); OAuth2 applies only to the MCP connector, so OAuth2-only is wrong.
A documented public REST API with API keys exists, so it is buildable — but only for Enterprise workspaces, making needs_partner_or_paid_access the right verdict rather than not_buildable_today.

qa_table.original: No public API or API keys; only vendor-sanctioned surface is hosted OAuth MCP server (plan-gated)
qa_table.verified_value: Public API and API keys exist but are gated to Enterprise workspaces (account-manager enablement); no self-serve developer access on lower plans
qa_table.notes: The claim 'no public API or API keys' is now factually wrong per Otter's own Public API article; the real blocker is Enterprise-plan gating.
Official Otter MCP confirmed via Otter's own blog and the help-center article cited by the first pass; OAuth-authenticated and gated to Business/Enterprise plans.

Consensus API access is by application only with custom per-call quotes (~$0.10/call baseline plus platform fee) and a waitlist — no self-serve developer signup; the MCP connector is self-serve but the API is not.
docs.consensus.app hosts an API Reference with HTTP endpoints and per-call pricing consistent with a REST API; no contradicting evidence found.
Consensus docs show API access uses an API key sent as 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY' (OAuth is used for the hosted MCP in mainstream clients), so auth is documented rather than unclear.
API keys require an approved application and custom-priced agreement, so building on the raw API needs partner/paid access (the free-account MCP path exists but is not general API access).

qa_table.original: API credentials require application/approval and custom per-call pricing; no self-serve developer signup
qa_table.verified_value: API credentials require application/approval and custom per-call pricing; no self-serve developer signup
qa_table.notes: Confirmed application-only access with waitlist and custom quotes (~$0.10/call baseline), matching the stated blocker.
Official docs page describes the vendor-hosted MCP server at mcp.consensus.app/mcp with OAuth for Claude/ChatGPT/Cursor clients — official_mcp confirmed.
